primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma with ulceration and haemorrhage

comorbidities.name: Chronic photodamage / actinic keratosis at site

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic UV damage drives keratinocyte transformation; here a pre-existing actinic keratosis on the sun-exposed dorsal hand has progressed over weeks into an invasive cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma — a firm, deeply indurated, keratotic (cutaneous horn) nodule that has ulcerated and bled, with fragile tumour vessels and apixaban prolonging the haemorrhage. The lesion feels tethered to deeper tissue and there is a palpable ipsilateral axillary node, raising concern for regional metastasis (SCC, unlike BCC, can metastasise). Discriminators: rapid growth, thick keratotic scale and deep induration favour SCC over a slow-growing pearly BCC; the asymmetrical, deeply indurated, ulcerated architecture (rather than a symmetrical dome with a central keratin plug) argues against keratoacanthoma; and the deep nodular induration with nodal involvement means it has evolved beyond a superficial actinic keratosis. Melanoma is not the lead given the keratotic morphology, but any pigmented change would warrant its own assessment. A telehealth provider cannot cauterise or biopsy: the correct actions are urgent in-person haemostasis (firm prolonged pressure, elevation; silver nitrate/alginate/cautery in person) and an urgent fast-track surgical/oncology referral for excision and nodal evaluation, with an ED safety-net for uncontrolled bleeding.

differential.evidence_basis: Rapidly growing indurated keratotic ulcerated nodule on sun-damaged skin, with a regional node.
Rapid growth but usually a symmetrical dome with central keratin plug; deep induration/ulceration favour SCC.
Slow-growing pearly lesion — the rapid keratotic indurated growth argues against BCC.
Superficial precancer; the deep nodular induration means it has progressed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a high-risk suspected SCC (with a regional node), avoid false reassurance, arrange urgent haemostasis + fast-track surgical/oncology referral.

differential.diagnosis: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma (suspected, possible nodal spread)
differential.evidence_basis: Indurated ulcerated keratotic nodule + palpable regional node — cannot be excluded remotely; needs urgent excision/staging.
Even if KA, it is excised/treated as SCC — histology decides.
Working assessment: urgent in-person haemostasis + fast-track surgical/oncology referral for excision and nodal evaluation.

red_flags.description: Rapidly growing, indurated, ulcerated keratotic nodule on sun-damaged skin (high-risk suspected SCC)
red_flags.clinical_significance: High-risk keratinocyte cancer requiring urgent excision.

red_flags.description: Palpable regional (axillary) lymph node — possible metastasis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests regional spread; mandates urgent staging and surgical/oncology input.
Significant haemorrhage risk needing in-person haemostasis; ED if uncontrolled.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing a bleeding indurated nodule as benign/traumatic and not arranging urgent excision.
Not recognising the palpable regional node as a metastasis concern needing staging.
Not arranging urgent haemostasis for uncontrolled bleeding on apixaban.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent in-person haemostasis (firm prolonged pressure, elevation; cautery/alginate in person) and an urgent fast-track surgical/oncology referral for excision and nodal evaluation; ED if bleeding uncontrolled.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: False reassurance/delay for a high-risk bleeding SCC, or under-managing the anticoagulated bleeding.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Cancer Council Australia keratinocyte cancer guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: High-risk SCC (rapid growth, induration, size, immunosuppression, nodal disease) requires urgent excision with histology and staging; assess regional nodes.
